variables.content: Given finite-size effective-mass data points \\(\\{(N_{i},m^{*}(N_{i}))\\}\\) at several densities and considering the family of fits \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) parametrized by \\(\\gamma\\), the proposition is: adopting a fixed exponent value \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolations across the densities considered is justified because the resulting fits produce substantially better overall goodness-of-fit statistics as measured by the reduced \\(\\chi^{2}\\) values than alternative fixed exponents over the same data sets, and therefore using \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields reliable extrapolated values \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) for these DMC data.

The finite-size dependence of the computed quasiparticle effective mass was modeled for the available DMC data by the empirical single-power-law scaling ansatz
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      where \\(m^{*}(N)\\) is the effective mass obtained for a finite periodic simulation containing \\(N\\) electrons, \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denotes the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective mass, and \\(b\\) and \\(\\gamma\\) are fit parameters. Repeated fits with Monte Carlo sampling of the statistical error bars on the input finite-\\(N\\) effective masses yield fitted exponents \\(\\gamma=1.8(4)\\), \\(1.4(4)\\), and \\(1.4(3)\\) at density parameters \\(r_{s}=1\\), 5, and 10, respectively, indicating that the optimal exponent lies between 1 and 2. For the purpose of extrapolations reported here the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) was adopted because it gives good goodness-of-fit statistics (reduced \\(\\chi^{2}\\)) across the densities considered.

Systematic finite-size biases in the DMC effective mass m* can be eliminated by the following protocol: compute DMC total-energy differences to obtain quasiparticle energies at multiple momenta k in the interval 0 ≤ k < 2 k_F for a sequence of finite periodic simulation-cell electron numbers N (paramagnetic N up to 274 electrons were used); fit the computed interacting band values E(k) at the sampled k points to a smooth rational (Padé) function of the form E_fit(k) = (A_0 + A_1 k + A_2 k^2 + A_3^2 k^3)/(1 + 2 A_3^2 k), where A_0, A_1, A_2, and A_3 are real fit parameters determined by least-squares fitting; extract the finite-N effective mass m*(N) = k_F / (dE_fit/dk)_{k_F} from the fitted band; and extrapolate m*(N) linearly as a function of 1/N to N → ∞ to obtain the thermodynamic-limit m*. Cross-checks performed with quartic polynomial fits to E(k) show that the choice between the Padé form and quartic polynomials has a negligible effect on the extrapolated m* values for the densities and spin polarizations studied.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

Fitting the system-size dependence of the finite-N quasiparticle effective mass to the power-law form m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} (with N the simulation-cell electron count) yields optimal exponents γ in the range 1≲γ≲2 for the accessible N values; specifically, numerical least-squares fits with Monte Carlo sampling of the quoted error bars give γ=1.8(4), 1.4(4), and 1.4(3) at r_s=1, 5, and 10, respectively, indicating that the observed finite-size scaling of m^*(N) in these DMC data is characterized by a power law with 1≲γ≲2 rather than the N^{-1/4} behavior predicted for excitations infinitesimally close to k_F.

The computed quasiparticle effective mass m* for paramagnetic 2D-UEL exhibits a systematic dependence on the simulation-cell electron number N that is empirically well described by the form m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} for some constant β; applying this N^{-3/2} empirical scaling to finite-N VMC and DMC data (for example N = 146, 218, 302) permits controlled extrapolation to estimates of m*(∞) in the thermodynamic limit. Observationally in the reported datasets, finite-size errors of m* are smaller in VMC than in DMC and the finite-size errors grow as the density is reduced (larger r_s).

Let $E_b^{\mathrm{DMC}}(\gamma)$ denote the infinite-system barrier obtained by a two-point extrapolation of $\Delta\bar{E}_{\mathrm{sp-fs}}^{\mathrm{DMC}}(N)$ using an assumed leading many-body finite-size scaling $D^{\mathrm{mb-fs}}(N)\propto N^{\gamma}$ with $N$ proportional to the number of explicitly correlated particles/electrons. Replacing the exponent $\gamma=-5/4$ by $\gamma=-3/2$ in this two-point extrapolation changes the extrapolated DMC barrier by only $0.1\ \mathrm{kcal/mol}$ for the system sizes used.

A practical protocol for extracting quasiparticle bands and effective masses in finite-size QMC calculations is: (i) compute the single-particle energy $\mathcal{E}(\mathbf{k})$ as the difference of many-body total energies obtained by adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$ (instead of promoting an electron within the $N$-electron configuration), thereby avoiding extra contributions to the energy differences from interactions between an excited electron and the hole it leaves behind; and (ii) perform a least-squares fit of a smooth quartic function $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ to the DMC band values over the entire occupied range and evaluate the derivative of that fitted function at $k_{F}$ to obtain the effective mass, which reduces sensitivity to Hartree-Fock-like residual pathological fluctuations and finite-size irregularities in the immediate vicinity of $k_{F}$.  Using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that were optimized in the $N$-electron ground state and then used without reoptimization in the $(N\pm1)$-electron DMC calculations produces stable band values and effective masses that are insensitive to system-size trends that appear when only near-$k_{F}$ points are employed.

We implemented a fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (FN–DMC) protocol for the homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) of N electrons interacting via the pair potential $v(r)=2/(r_{s}r)$ in a square box of side $L=\sqrt{N\pi}$ with periodic boundary conditions and a neutralizing uniform positive background, in which computed DMC energies are extrapolated to zero imaginary time step ($\tau\to0$), to infinite walker population ($N_{W}\to\infty$), and to the thermodynamic limit ($N\to\infty$) by fitting finite-$N$ FN–DMC energies at several polarizations $\zeta=(N_{\uparrow}-N_{\downarrow})/N$ and particle numbers $N$ to the functional form
      $$
      E_{N}(\zeta)=E(\zeta=0)+\beta\zeta^{2}+\gamma\zeta^{4}+\Delta(1+\epsilon\zeta^{2})\bigl(T_{N}^{(0)}(\zeta)-T^{(0)}(\zeta)\bigr)-(b+\alpha\zeta^{2})/N,
      $$
      where $E_{N}(\zeta)$ is the FN–DMC energy per particle for $N$ electrons at polarization $\zeta$, $T_{N}^{(0)}(\zeta)$ is the noninteracting kinetic energy for $N$ particles at density parameter $r_{s}=1$, $T^{(0)}(\zeta)$ is the corresponding thermodynamic-limit kinetic energy, and $\beta,\gamma,\Delta,\epsilon,b,\alpha$ are fit parameters.  For the plane-wave-node FN–DMC data the fitted parameters found here are, at $r_{s}=20$: $E(\zeta=0)=-0.092456\,$Ry, $\beta=8.22\times10^{-5}\,$Ry, $\gamma=-7.34\times10^{-3}\,$Ry, $\Delta=0.00129$, $\epsilon=2.0$, $b=0.00712$, $\alpha=-0.0030$ (fit quality $x^{2}=1.7$); and at $r_{s}=30$: $E(\zeta=0)=-0.063783\,$Ry, $\beta=-6.4\times10^{-6}\,$Ry, $\gamma=-2.72\times10^{-3}\,$Ry, $\Delta=0.00045$, $\epsilon=1.8$, $b=0.00397$, $\alpha=-0.0007$ ($x^{2}=1.9$). Using this protocol we obtain, for the case $N=58$ at $r_{s}=20$, an extrapolated FN–DMC energy $-0.092557(76)\,$Ry, which can be compared with earlier published values $-0.09248(1)\,$Ry reported for the same system; this implementation differs from those earlier works in that (i) the number ($N$) extrapolation is performed directly by fitting several DMC energies at different $N$ rather than by applying variational Monte Carlo size corrections to a single DMC size, and (ii) explicit extrapolations in imaginary time step $\tau$ and walker number $N_{W}$ are performed to reduce finite-$\tau$ and population-control biases.

Extrapolating the two single-particle-finite-size-corrected DMC barrier estimates $\Delta \bar{E}_{\text{sp-fs}}^{\text{DMC}}(2\times2) = 14.8\pm0.7\ \text{kcal/mol}$ and $\Delta \bar{E}_{\text{sp-fs}}^{\text{DMC}}(4\times4) = 13.3\pm0.8\ \text{kcal/mol}$ to the infinite-system limit using a two-point fit to $\Delta \bar{E}_{\text{sp-fs}}^{\text{DMC}}(N)=E_b^{\text{DMC}}+c\,N^{-5/4}$ (with $N$ proportional to the number of explicitly correlated particles/electrons in the simulation cell) yields $E_{b}^{\text{DMC}} = 13.0 \pm 1.0\ \text{kcal/mol}$, and replacing the exponent $-5/4$ by $-3/2$ changes the extrapolated $E_b^{\text{DMC}}$ by only $0.1\ \text{kcal/mol}$.
